Purim Meshulash — Purim Meshulash is the rare Jerusalem schedule when Shushan Purim (15 Adar) falls on Shabbat. Megillah and matanot l'evyonim move to Friday; mishloach manot and the seudah move to Sunday. Shabbat carries communal Purim obligations — special Torah reading (Vayavo Amalek), Haftarah, and Al HaNissim in Amidah and bentching (not on Friday or Sunday).

Serve Hashem with Simcha (Joy)! 😊 This isn't just a nice idea; Rabbi Nachman of Breslov (Likutei Moharan I, 24) teaches that performing mitzvot with joy elevates them to the highest spiritual levels, connecting them to the Keter (the 'crown' of creation)! He emphasized that joy is the key to spiritual breakthroughs and moving forward in life when you feel stuck. Think of it: a mitzvah done with a smile, song, or even a little dance is far more impactful! Your mission today: choose one mitzvah and perform it with extra happiness – whether it's giving tzedakah with a cheerful heart, singing during Torah study, or truly celebrating after prayer. Rabbi Nachman taught that joy isn't optional; it's how we access profound spiritual growth and closeness to Hashem! Source: Likutei Moharan I, 24; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

Shacharit is the morning service — Pesukei d'Zimra, Shema and its blessings, the Amidah, and on Shabbat and Yom Tov Torah reading and Musaf. The Shema must be recited before the end of the third halachic hour; the Amidah ideally before the end of the fourth. Tefillin are worn on weekdays. Daily Shacharit anchors the day in prayer before the world's noise.

The Shema must be recited before the end of the third halachic hour; the Amidah ideally before the end of the fourth. Tefillin are worn on weekdays. The Fast of 17 Tammuz (Shiva Asar B'Tammuz) marks the day the walls of Jerusalem were breached by the Romans in the first century CE. It begins the "Three Weeks" — a period of mourning culminating in Tisha B'Av.

It is a minor fast from dawn until nightfall. During the Three Weeks, weddings are not held and music is generally avoided. During the final nine days of this period (1–9 Av), additional mourning customs apply.

Uncover the good point — practice Azamra! 🎵 This is one of Rabbi Nachman of Breslov's most transformative teachings: even in a person who seems completely lost or flawed, you *must* search until you find a single spark of goodness within them. By focusing on that good point, judging them favorably, you can actually help elevate both them and yourself spiritually! 'Azamra' means 'I will sing,' because by finding and celebrating that hidden good, you create a melody of praise to Hashem. Try this powerful exercise today: pick someone (or even look within yourself!) and consciously identify one genuinely positive quality. Watch how this shift in perspective can transform your entire outlook! Source: Likutei Moharan I, 282; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

Want to be a superstar kid (or adult)? 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Discover the incredibly important mitzvah of Kibud Av Va'em (Honoring your Father and Mother)! It's one of the Ten Commandments, and it's more than just saying 'thank you.' It's about deep respect, ensuring their comfort, caring for them as they age, and bringing them nachas (joy/pride) through your actions. This mitzvah teaches gratitude, humility, and recognizing the chain of tradition. What's super cool? The Gemara (Kiddushin 31a) teaches that there's NO limit to honoring parents – every moment is an opportunity! Even if they've passed on, we honor them by learning Torah, performing mitzvot, and dedicating acts of kindness in their memory. So, think of one specific act you can do today to make your parents proud, whether they're with you or in the Olam Haba (World to Come)!
elegies read on Tisha B'Av mourning the Temple

levi — Levi is the tribe serving in the Temple; today leviim are often called to the Torah after kohanim. Many carry the family name Levi or related traditions. Kohanim and leviim have other halachot (priestly blessing, tumah). If unsure of your status, ask family and your rav.
